subject: [PATCH v6 00/15] userfaultfd: working set tracking for VM guest memory

2026-06-02.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for tracking the working set of VM guest memory using userfaultfd, allowing a VMM to identify cold pages and evict them to slower storage. The approach uses a new registration mode (MODE_RWP) that marks tracked pages as inaccessible while keeping them resident, and delivers a UFFD_PAGEFAULT_FLAG_RWP message to the registered handler in sync mode or auto-resolves faults in async mode. This enables efficient memory management for VM guests by detecting which pages are still being accessed and evicting cold ones to slower storage.

reviews.author: Askar Safin
reviews.summary: Reviewer Askar Safin noted that their recent patch, which made vmsplice equivalent to preadv2/pwritev2 and was accepted for next, is an experiment that may be reverted, and requested the userfaultfd series to consider this change as a potential precedent.
reviews.raw_body:
Note that I recently submitted a patch, which makes vmsplice equivalent to
preadv2/pwritev2, and it was accepted to next.

For now it is just an experiment, it is possible it will be reverted.

https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260601-aufweichen-dissens-ausrechnen-0d9b84728113@brauner/

-- 
Askar Safin

reviews.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
reviews.summary:
Gave Reviewed-by

The reviewer suggested using vma_flags_test_single_mask() instead of vma_test_any_mask() for clarity, but noted it was not a significant issue.
reviews.raw_body:
NIT: You could use vma_flags_test_single_mask() just to make it clear it's a single
flag. It's not a big deal though really.

reviews.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
reviews.summary:
Gave Reviewed-by

Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes suggested a simplification to the existing code by replacing two conditions with a single call to `vma_test_single_mask()`

2026-06-03.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for tracking the working set of VM guest memory using userfaultfd, allowing a VMM to identify cold pages and evict them to slower storage. The approach uses a new registration mode (MODE_RWP) that marks tracked pages as inaccessible while keeping them resident, and delivers a UFFD_PAGEFAULT_FLAG_RWP message to the registered handler in sync mode or auto-resolves faults in async mode. This enables efficient memory management for VM guests by detecting which pages are still being accessed and evicting cold ones to slower storage.
